title: Reverse a C-style String
prompt_md:
## Goal
Read a word into a C-style string, report its length, then print the characters in reverse order as a new C-style string.

## Input
- A single line containing one word (no spaces, at most 40 characters).

## Output
- Line 1: the length of the input string (as reported by `std::strlen`)
- Line 2: the string with its characters in reverse order

## Requirements
1. Declare a `char` array of size 50 to hold the input. Use `std::cin.getline()` to read the line.
2. Use `std::strlen` (from `<cstring>`) to compute the length.
3. Declare a second `char` array of size 50. Fill it with the characters of the input in reverse order, then manually place a null terminator at the correct position.
4. Print the reversed array using `std::cout <<`.
5. Do not use `std::string`, `std::reverse`, or any `<algorithm>` function.

## Example
Input: `hello` produces length `5` and reversed string `olleh`.

title: Compare and Concatenate C-style Strings
prompt_md:
## Goal
Read two words into C-style strings, determine their lexicographic order using `std::strcmp`, then concatenate them and print the result along with its length.

## Input
- Line 1: a single word (no spaces, at most 30 characters)
- Line 2: a single word (no spaces, at most 30 characters)

## Output
- Line 1: one of three words describing the order of the first string relative to the second:
  - `before` if the first string comes before the second lexicographically
  - `after` if the first string comes after the second
  - `equal` if both strings are identical
- Line 2: the length of the concatenated string (first + second, no separator)
- Line 3: the concatenated string itself

## Requirements
1. Declare two `char` arrays of size 64 for the inputs. Use `std::cin.getline()` to read each.
2. Use `std::strcmp` to compare the two strings.
3. Declare a third `char` array large enough to hold both strings concatenated. Use `std::strcpy` to copy the first string into it, then `std::strcat` to append the second.
4. Use `std::strlen` to get the length of the concatenated result.
5. Do not use `std::string` or any `<algorithm>` function.

## Example
Inputs `apple` and `banana` produces `before`, length `11`, and concatenation `applebanana`.
